name: Business Viability

validated_findings.finding: There is a plausible business wedge around organizers who need trust-building and coordination around group trips, but willingness to pay has not been established.

evidence.summary: Stage 1 identifies organizer activation, contribution conversion, expense transparency, receipt usability, and member participation as success criteria.

validated_findings.finding: Payment-related revenue should not be part of MVP assumptions because public contribution checkout and payout are delegated to an external provider.

evidence.summary: Stripe charge type affects whether platform or connected accounts are debited for fees, refunds, disputes, and chargebacks, supporting the decision to avoid Crewly-owned funds flow in MVP.
GoFundMe emphasizes no fee to start fundraising, simple pricing, and trust and safety, indicating contributor fee perception is part of the product trust model.

business_constraints: Do not assume transaction margin from public contributions in MVP.
External provider terms, fees, and data availability must be validated before implementation planning.
Do not rely on broad social virality before proving organizer activation and contribution conversion.
Keep early monetization optional or subscription-oriented unless payment economics are validated.
scalability_constraints: Receipt OCR, translation, media storage, and exchange-rate lookups introduce variable usage cost.
Moderation and payment support load can scale faster than revenue if public pages and organizer payouts are loosely controlled.
recommendations: Run willingness-to-pay and contribution fee sensitivity tests before deciding monetization.
Model cost per active trip, cost per receipt, and payment support cost before Stage 5 ticketing.
Keep MVP scope constrained to reduce operational cost exposure.
